Return on ad spend for many of you is the biggest KPI that you're aiming for. So for you, higher the ROAS, the profitability, and the more you can invest in your ad to scale up. For other people, ROAS isn't the biggest factor. It's things like lifetime value. So to give you an example, if you're in the subscription space, if you're in e-com or services-based subscription, often the cost per acquisition is far more important than return on ad spend because you're looking at lifetime value. But even for single purchase products, often lifetime value is more important than ROAS. However, specifically for ROAS, as we've already discovered, it's a factor of how much you're paying to acquire the customer, your CPA, and what your average order value is. And what I want to touch on here is how to kind of work on your average order value in particular because one of the things that I try and get people to understand is, you know, we've covered this already. When it comes to shifting your CPA and your AOV, your average order value is usually much easier to influence. It just needs a few ideas to think about what you can do to change the value of the customer transaction. So number one is the price. I don't think enough people do pricing experiments to try and find the point where you're generating a higher profit and high return on ad spend without impacting your conversion rate. So for example, when you're selling a product, let's say you've just launched a product, it's selling for $39.99 on the store, and you know, you're generating a cost per acquisition of let's say $30. That leaves you a $10 margin for your product. And if your product costs $20, you're going to end up in the negative. So you can either now try and get your cost per acquisition down from $30 to say $20, or you can just bump up your average order value by playing with the different elements. So for example, price, depending on the type of product and the type of niche and who you're kind of aiming for and kind of what that experience looks like, you might be able to shift that price up. Now the hardest thing to do is once you've established your store, your products, etc., is to shift the price. But if there's a reason to do so, then it's easier to get across. So for example, if you have limited stock, limited availability, you can play around with the price and bring that feature out on your Lando in your copy, in your ads, for example, and not playing with fake scarcity. One of the things about the kind of consumers is trust and kind of brand relationship go a long, long way. So if you are creating that fake scarcity, then that loyalty is unlikely to remain. So when it comes to price testing, there are a few different ways that you can do this. So the easiest would be that you shift the price of your product for your top of funnel audience. But what you do is for your retargeting audience, you potentially offer them a discount to offset any price disparity. So for example, we had a recent project where we had to bump up the price of an Econ product. I think it was from about $45 to $50 because the shipping cost went up. So we had two choices. Number one, we switch from free shipping to paid shipping. So we could have just said shipping is now $5. But for this one, we actually increased the price and kept the shipping free. And we did that by bumping up the price on the store. So anyone new coming into the landing page would have seen the new price. So they're unaffected by anything that's happened before. But for anyone that's not transacted in the first day, on the second day, we counted it with a discount code. Now the reason for that is for this particular store, we know that most people convert on the first day. So if cold traffic comes in and they see the price for the first time and they buy, then we know things are working fine. If they don't buy after day one, so this is people that have already seen the original price shift, then we would retarget them and offer them a discount just to reduce that price back down. And one of the things that you want to do is when you're doing price testing, to be really mindful of what the consumer has already seen and what you're presenting across. Now $5 shift in price isn't such a big deal. I'll give you another example. We have another product which is Info Products, it sells for $197. We are looking to shift that up to $297. The way we would test that in particular is to create a second variant of that product page. So for example, if you're selling an Ecom, an Info product, it doesn't matter and you are introducing a new price, often what you can do is duplicate that product page or the service or whatever you're selling with the higher price and only offer that to new cold audiences to test that against what's going on right now. So using Facebook's split test, I would run that as a split test. So you take a single audience, you split that $50.50 and you push that test to either of those pages so that Facebook will only ever send one group of users to one landing page and you can literally just measure the difference in performance. So conversion rate, the return on ad spend for example, because it could be that your cost per acquisition goes up. That's one of the kind of considerations for price testing. Then we talk about, right, okay, so if price is fixed or we've been through that process already, what else can we do? There's bundles, there's promotions, there's upsells, there's cross-sells, one-time offers and funnels. And in the next video, I'm going to show you the different types of landing pages, different types of ways of influencing people to increase that return on ad spend, both within Ecom and on the info product side, because when you are scaling up, often what you'll find is your return on ad spend may fluctuate. But if you're in a niche which is highly competitive and working on the Facebook side of things is more difficult. So for example, in the info product space, CPMs are generally a lot more higher, A, because the level of competition and B, Facebook don't seem to favor it as much as Ecom, for example. So if you're in the info product space, you're getting $30, $40, $50 CPMs, which can be quite normal, then reducing your cost per acquisition could be tougher. You could increase your conversion rate, you could work on decrease in your CPM, but often the AOV is low hanging fruit and far easier to influence. So do consider when you're going through your campaigns, what you can work on to bump up your ROAS. Now, for our agency clients, for example, one of the things that we find is that shifting the ROAS has a bigger impact than anything else we do. So if you look at your strategies as a tool set, so going back to your ad account performance, how can you improve your metrics? So one of them could be that your ROAS remains fixed, but you work on lifetime value. So over time, once you've acquired that customer once, then you get them to repurchase via email and via other channels so that the Facebook acquisition cost remains fixed. So it might cost you, for example, in this first example, we've got an average order value of $40, a cost per acquisition of $40, which means your return on Aspen is 100%, which for most businesses is a loss, which means you've spent $1, you made $1 back, but considering the cost involved with selling, producing, etc., is a negative. But if over time that customer buys two, three, four, five products, let's say over the next 12, 24 months, the cost per acquisition remains the same. And if you're using email to reacquire, then your cost per acquisition becomes that much more profitable. So rather than your first sale generating $40, five sales later, you've generated over $200. Now the return on Aspen or the return on investment is that much higher. So when you're kind of thinking about your business right now, think about where your leverage is. So can you maximize your return on Aspen on the first purchase so that you can increase your profits? Or are you playing a mid and long term game where you're not going to worry so much about return on Aspen and therefore you're going to scale up even though your return on Aspen isn't as strong, but you're going to get your payback in two months, in three months, in six months time. And that's a big factor in deciding how then you tackle that front end. So we talk about prices, bundles, promotions, etc. You need to know your numbers first. So really kind of right up at the start of the BPM method training, we were talking about setting your targets and your KPIs. It's so, so important to have that in place before you jump into your optimizations because without a destination, it's hard to find a path. And this is the biggest thing about optimization. So have your destination. Now if ROAS is the thing that you want to influence right now, then this is what we'll be focusing on. So in the subsequent video, I'm going to show you some different styles of working on average order value to give you some ideas on what you can apply into your business right now.
